question_text: Dissolution of a partnership firm means:
explanation: Dissolution of a firm means the complete closure of the business — all accounts are settled, assets are sold, liabilities are paid, and the firm ceases to exist. This is different from dissolution of partnership (reconstitution) where the firm continues under a changed composition.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

options.text: Change in the existing agreement among partners
Complete winding up of the business of the firm
Admission of a new partner into the firm
Retirement of one partner from the firm

elimination_hints.hint: Change in agreement is reconstitution, not dissolution. In dissolution, the firm itself ceases to exist.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing reconstitution with dissolution.

elimination_hints.hint: Admission is a form of reconstitution — the firm continues with a new partner. Dissolution ends the firm.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing admission with dissolution.

elimination_hints.hint: Retirement changes the partnership composition but the firm continues. Dissolution means the firm stops altogether.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing retirement (reconstitution) with dissolution.

question_text: Which of the following is NOT a mode of dissolution of a firm?
explanation: A firm can be dissolved by: (i) agreement among all partners, (ii) compulsory dissolution (illegality), (iii) on happening of certain contingencies, (iv) by notice, (v) by court order. Admission of a new partner is reconstitution — the firm continues, it does not dissolve.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

options.text: By mutual agreement of all partners
By order of the court
By admission of a new partner
When the business becomes illegal

elimination_hints.hint: Mutual agreement is a valid mode — partners can unanimously decide to wind up the firm.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing that agreement-based dissolution exists.

elimination_hints.hint: Court-ordered dissolution is valid under Section 44 of the Partnership Act when certain conditions are met.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing about judicial dissolution.

elimination_hints.hint: If the business becomes illegal, dissolution is compulsory under Section 41. The firm cannot continue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking an illegal business can continue as a partnership.

question_text: Dissolution of partnership is different from dissolution of firm because:
explanation: In dissolution of partnership, the relationship between partners changes (e.g., one retires, one is admitted) but the firm continues its business. In dissolution of firm, the entire business is wound up — assets sold, liabilities paid, firm ceases to exist.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subtopic: Dissolution of partnership vs dissolution of firm
subject: accountancy

options.text: In dissolution of partnership, the firm continues; in dissolution of firm, the firm ceases
In dissolution of firm, the firm continues; in dissolution of partnership, the firm ceases
Both result in the firm ceasing to exist
There is no difference between the two

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the correct relationship. 'Dissolution of firm' = firm ceases. 'Dissolution of partnership' = firm continues.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the two concepts.

elimination_hints.hint: Only dissolution of firm ends the firm. Dissolution of partnership merely reconstitutes it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating both as the same.

elimination_hints.hint: There is a clear legal and practical distinction — one ends the firm, the other merely changes its composition.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between the two terms.

question_text: Under Section 41 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932, a firm is compulsorily dissolved when:
explanation: Section 41 provides for compulsory dissolution when all partners or all but one partner become insolvent, or when the business of the firm becomes illegal. In such cases, the firm MUST dissolve — there is no choice.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

options.text: One partner gives notice to other partners
A partner retires from the firm
Partners mutually agree to dissolve
The business of the firm becomes illegal

elimination_hints.hint: Notice-based dissolution is under Section 43 for partnership-at-will, not compulsory dissolution.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing voluntary notice with compulsory dissolution.

elimination_hints.hint: Retirement is reconstitution. The firm can continue with remaining partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing retirement with compulsory dissolution.

elimination_hints.hint: Mutual agreement is voluntary dissolution, not compulsory. Compulsory means there's no choice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing voluntary with compulsory dissolution.

question_text: Under Section 44 of the Partnership Act, a court may order dissolution of a firm on which of the following grounds?
explanation: Section 44 lists grounds on which the court may dissolve a firm: (a) partner becomes of unsound mind, (b) partner becomes permanently incapable, (c) partner guilty of misconduct, (d) partner persistently breaches agreement, (e) partner transfers their interest, (f) business can only run at a loss, (g) court finds it just and equitable. A partner's temporary illness is not a ground.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

options.text: Temporary illness of a partner
A partner becoming of unsound mind
Disagreement on minor business decisions
One partner wanting a higher profit share

elimination_hints.hint: Temporary illness is not a ground. Only permanent incapacity is listed under Section 44.
elimination_hints.misconception: Equating temporary illness with permanent incapacity.

elimination_hints.hint: Minor disagreements are normal in business. Only persistent breach of agreement qualifies.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating routine disagreements as dissolution grounds.

elimination_hints.hint: Profit share disputes are settled through agreement, not dissolution. This is not a Section 44 ground.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating financial disagreements as dissolution grounds.

question_text: In a partnership-at-will, the firm may be dissolved:
explanation: In a partnership-at-will (no fixed term, no specific purpose), any partner can dissolve the firm by giving written notice to all other partners. The dissolution takes effect from the date mentioned in the notice, or from the date of communication if no date is mentioned (Section 43).
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

options.text: Only by mutual agreement of all partners
Only by court order
By any partner giving notice in writing to all other partners
Only when the business becomes illegal

elimination_hints.hint: Mutual agreement works for any partnership. But a partnership-at-will has an additional option — notice by any one partner.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing about the special notice provision.

elimination_hints.hint: Court order is needed only for specific grounds under Section 44. A partnership-at-will can be dissolved simply by notice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking court order is always needed.

elimination_hints.hint: Illegality is compulsory dissolution (Section 41). Partnership-at-will has a simpler route — written notice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing compulsory dissolution with at-will dissolution.

question_text: The correct order of settlement of accounts on dissolution of a firm (as per Section 48) is:
explanation: Section 48 prescribes the order: (1) Losses are paid from profits, then capital, then by partners individually in profit-sharing ratio. (2) Assets are applied: first to pay third-party liabilities, then partners' loans, then partners' capital, then surplus (if any) in profit-sharing ratio.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

options.text: Third-party liabilities → Partners' loans → Partners' capital → Surplus in PSR
Partners' capital → Third-party liabilities → Partners' loans → Surplus
Partners' loans → Third-party liabilities → Partners' capital → Surplus
Third-party liabilities → Partners' capital → Partners' loans → Surplus

elimination_hints.hint: Third-party creditors have the highest priority — they must be paid before any partner. Capital comes last.
elimination_hints.misconception: Prioritizing partners over external creditors.

elimination_hints.hint: Partners' loans cannot come before third-party liabilities. External creditors always have first claim.
elimination_hints.misconception: Prioritizing partners' loans over external debts.

elimination_hints.hint: Partners' loans have priority over partners' capital. A partner acting as a lender is treated differently from a partner as an owner.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between partner's loan and partner's capital.

question_text: Every dissolution of partnership results in dissolution of the firm.
explanation: FALSE. Dissolution of partnership means a change in the relationship among partners (e.g., retirement, admission, death). The firm may or may not dissolve. But dissolution of the firm always involves dissolution of the partnership. The converse is not true.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subtopic: Relationship between dissolution of partnership and firm
subject: accountancy

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: Think of retirement — the partnership dissolves (composition changes) but the firm continues with remaining partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Equating partnership dissolution with firm dissolution.

question_text: Dissolution of a firm always results in dissolution of the partnership.
explanation: TRUE. When a firm dissolves (winds up completely), the partnership relationship automatically ends. There can be no partnership without a firm. Hence, dissolution of firm necessarily includes dissolution of partnership.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subtopic: Relationship between dissolution of partnership and firm
subject: accountancy

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: If the firm ceases to exist, the partnership must end too. You can't have a partnership without a firm.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking partnership can continue after the firm dissolves.

question_text: On dissolution, partners' loans to the firm are repaid before third-party liabilities.
explanation: FALSE. Third-party liabilities (external creditors) have the first priority on dissolution. Partners' loans are repaid after all external liabilities are settled. This protects outsiders who dealt with the firm in good faith.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: External creditors always come first. Partners' loans rank second. This is per Section 48.
elimination_hints.misconception: Giving partners priority over external creditors.

question_text: On dissolution of a firm, losses are first met out of _______, then out of _______, and lastly by partners individually.
explanation: As per Section 48, losses are paid: first from profits (if any), then from capital of partners, and lastly if needed, by partners individually in their profit-sharing ratio. This graduated approach protects partners from personal liability until all other sources are exhausted.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

question_text: In a partnership-at-will, the firm may be dissolved by any partner giving _______ to all other partners.
explanation: Under Section 43, any partner in a partnership-at-will can dissolve the firm by giving notice in writing to all other partners. The dissolution takes effect from the date specified in the notice or the date of communication.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

question_text:
Assertion (A): On dissolution of a firm, external liabilities are paid before partners' loans.
Reason (R): Partners, being owners, have a residual claim on the firm's assets.

Choose the correct option:
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. External creditors lent money or supplied goods in good faith. Partners, as owners, have a residual (last) claim on the assets. This is consistent with the legal principle that creditors are protected before owners.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The residual nature of ownership directly explains why partners are paid after external creditors.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting residual ownership with payment priority.

elimination_hints.hint: R is a valid legal and financial principle — owners have residual claims, creditors have prior claims.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding the concept of residual claim.

elimination_hints.hint: A is the established rule under Section 48 — external liabilities first, then partners' loans.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the settlement order.

question_text:
Assertion (A): A firm constituted for a fixed term is automatically dissolved on the expiry of that term.
Reason (R): Section 42 of the Partnership Act provides for dissolution on the happening of certain contingencies.

Choose the correct option:
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Section 42 states that a firm is dissolved: (a) on expiry of the fixed term, (b) on completion of the specific venture/undertaking, (c) on death of a partner, (d) on insolvency of a partner. Expiry of fixed term is one such contingency.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Section 42 specifically covers the contingency of fixed term expiry — it IS the legal basis (explanation) for A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing that Section 42 directly covers this case.

elimination_hints.hint: R correctly identifies Section 42 as the provision for contingency-based dissolution. It is true.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing about Section 42.

elimination_hints.hint: A is true — a fixed-term partnership dissolves when the term expires unless renewed by agreement.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking a fixed-term partnership continues automatically.

question_text: Which of the following is NOT a ground for dissolution by court under Section 44?
explanation: Section 44 grounds include: unsound mind, permanent incapacity, misconduct, persistent breach, transfer of interest, business only at a loss, just and equitable. Loss in one particular year is NOT a ground — the court requires the business to be one that can ONLY be carried on at a loss (continuous/permanent losses).
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subtopic: Grounds not valid for court dissolution
subject: accountancy

options.text: A partner has become of permanently unsound mind
A partner has transferred their entire interest to a third party
The business can only be carried on at a loss
The firm suffered a loss in one particular financial year

elimination_hints.hint: Permanent unsoundness of mind is explicitly listed in Section 44(a). It IS a valid ground.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing Section 44 grounds.

elimination_hints.hint: Transfer of entire interest to a third party is covered under Section 44(e). It IS a valid ground.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing about the transfer ground.

elimination_hints.hint: 'Can only be carried on at a loss' is in Section 44(f). This refers to permanent inability to profit, not one bad year.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing permanent losses with temporary losses.

question_text: On dissolution, if a partner's capital account shows a debit balance (deficiency), and the partner is solvent, the partner must:
explanation: If a partner's capital account shows a debit balance after all adjustments, it means they owe money to the firm. If the partner is solvent, they must bring in cash to the extent of their deficiency to settle their account. This cash is used to pay off remaining liabilities or distribute to other partners.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

options.text: Bring in cash equal to the debit balance
Receive cash from other partners
Write off the debit balance as a loss
Transfer the deficit to the other partners' accounts in old ratio

elimination_hints.hint: A debit balance means the partner owes the firm, not the other way around. They bring cash in, not receive it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the direction of payment.

elimination_hints.hint: If the partner is solvent, the deficit cannot be written off. Only an insolvent partner's deficit may need special treatment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating a solvent partner as insolvent.

elimination_hints.hint: Transfer to other partners (Garner v Murray) applies only when a partner is insolvent. Solvent partners must pay up.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying insolvency rules to a solvent partner.

question_text: According to the Garner v Murray rule (as adapted in India), when an insolvent partner cannot pay their deficit, the loss is borne by solvent partners in their:
explanation: Under the Garner v Murray rule (as adapted in India), the insolvent partner's capital deficiency is borne by the remaining solvent partners in the ratio of their capital balances (as shown in the last agreed Balance Sheet, i.e., fixed capital balances), NOT in the profit-sharing ratio.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

options.text: Profit-sharing ratio
Equal ratio
Capital ratio (last agreed balance)
Gaining ratio

elimination_hints.hint: PSR is the default for sharing profits/losses, but Garner v Murray specifically mandates capital ratio for insolvency loss.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying PSR to all situations.

elimination_hints.hint: Equal ratio has no basis here. The rule specifically uses capital balances.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming equal sharing by default.

elimination_hints.hint: Gaining ratio applies to retirement goodwill, not dissolution insolvency. These are completely different events.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying retirement concepts to dissolution.

question_text:
Match the following Sections of the Indian Partnership Act with their provision:

| Column I (Section) | Column II (Provision) |
|---|---|
| (i) Section 40 | (p) Dissolution by notice (partnership-at-will) |
| (ii) Section 41 | (q) Dissolution by agreement |
| (iii) Section 43 | (r) Compulsory dissolution |
| (iv) Section 44 | (s) Dissolution by court order |

Choose the correct matching:
explanation: Section 40: Dissolution by agreement → (q). Section 41: Compulsory dissolution (illegality, insolvency of all/all but one) → (r). Section 43: Dissolution by notice in partnership-at-will → (p). Section 44: Dissolution by court order → (s).
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Section 40 is about agreement, not compulsory dissolution. Section 41 is compulsory.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping Sections 40 and 41.

elimination_hints.hint: Section 40 is agreement-based, not notice-based. Section 43 is for notice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing multiple sections.

elimination_hints.hint: Section 41 is compulsory (illegality), not court order. Section 44 is court order.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping Sections 41 and 44.

question_text: A, B, and C are partners sharing profits in 3:2:1. On dissolution, C is insolvent and cannot pay their deficit of ₹12,000. A's capital is ₹60,000 and B's capital is ₹40,000. Under Garner v Murray (Indian adaptation), how much of C's deficit does A bear?
explanation: Under Garner v Murray (Indian adaptation), the insolvent partner's deficit is borne by solvent partners in their capital ratio. A:B capital ratio = 60,000:40,000 = 3:2. A bears = 3/5 × ₹12,000 = ₹7,200.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹6,000 = 1/2 × 12,000 = equal sharing. But Garner v Murray uses capital ratio (3:2), not equal.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming equal sharing of insolvency loss.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹4,800 = 2/5 × 12,000 = B's share. This is what B bears, not A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing A's and B's shares.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹12,000 is the total deficit. A bears only 3/5 of it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assigning entire deficit to one partner.

question_text: Which of the following statements about dissolution of a firm is INCORRECT?
explanation: The incorrect statement is that on dissolution, assets are first used to repay partners' capital. The correct order (Section 48) is: (1) external liabilities, (2) partners' loans, (3) partners' capital, (4) surplus. Partners' capital comes third, not first.
topic: Types of Dissolution & Settlement of Accounts
subject: accountancy

options.text: On dissolution, third-party liabilities are paid first from the firm's assets
Partners' loans to the firm rank after third-party liabilities
On dissolution, assets are first used to repay partners' capital contributions
Any surplus after settlement is distributed among partners in profit-sharing ratio

elimination_hints.hint: This is correct — external creditors have the highest priority under Section 48.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking external creditors don't have priority.

elimination_hints.hint: This is correct — partners' loans come after external liabilities in the settlement order.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the settlement hierarchy.

elimination_hints.hint: This is correct — after all liabilities and capital are settled, any remaining surplus goes to partners in PSR.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing about surplus distribution.
